keyPoints: Operational capabilities matter after agent creation
Observability, cost control, governance, and harness design are durable priorities
whatILearned: The valuable enterprise work is moving from merely creating agents to operating them with visibility, cost control, governance, and reliable harnesses.
whyItMatters: Founder Build in Public needs trustworthy infrastructure around its model calls, including privacy gates, structured schemas, traceability, and cost metadata.

nextActions.text: Score the founder pipeline against observability, cost control, governance, and harness reliability

keyPoints: Learn emerging tools by building small experiments
Read release notes and monitor cost before trusting hype
whatILearned: AI experimentation is most useful when the hype is translated into small builds, checked against release notes, and bounded by cost and responsible-use controls.
whyItMatters: The hackathon demo should prove a narrow workflow honestly instead of making unsupported claims about broad AI capabilities.

nextActions.text: Keep fixture and live-provider claims visibly separated in the demo

keyPoints: Encode repeated expert decisions into an explicit guided path
Combine assessment, tasks, feedback, resources, and reminders into one journey
whatILearned: AI creates stronger product leverage when it codifies a proven expert workflow into a structured journey instead of offering an unbounded chat interface.
whyItMatters: Founder Build in Public follows the same pattern by turning an editorial workflow into explicit stages while preserving founder review and privacy gates.

nextActions.text: Identify the remaining editorial decisions that can become explicit pipeline stages without removing founder approval
